question:
Evaluate
$\displaystyle{\int_0^a\int_0^b e^{{\rm max}\{b^2x^2, a^2y^2\}}\,dy\,dx}$
where $a$ and $b$ are positive.

descriptive_long.question:
Evaluate
$\displaystyle{\int_0^{parametera}\int_0^{parameterb} e^{{\rm max}\{parameterb^{2} variablex^{2}, parametera^{2} variabley^{2}\}}\,d variabley\,d variablex}$
where $parametera$ and $parameterb$ are positive.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Evaluate
$\displaystyle{\int_0^{hummingbird}\int_0^{spectacular} e^{{\rm max}\{spectacular^2 lightning^2, hummingbird^2 waterfall^2\}}\,d waterfall\,d lightning}$
where $hummingbird$ and $spectacular$ are positive.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem:
<<<
Evaluate
$\displaystyle{\int_0^{negativealpha}\int_0^{negativebeta} e^{{\rm max}\{negativebeta^2 verticalaxis^2, negativealpha^2 horizontalaxis^2\}}\,d horizontalaxis\,d verticalaxis}$
where $negativealpha$ and $negativebeta$ are positive.
>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. Divide the rectangle into two parts by the diagonal line \( negativealpha\, horizontalaxis=negativebeta\, verticalaxis \) to obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{negativealpha} \int_{0}^{negativebeta} e^{\max \left\{negativebeta^{2} verticalaxis^{2}, negativealpha^{2} horizontalaxis^{2}\right\}} d horizontalaxis d verticalaxis & =\int_{0}^{negativealpha} \int_{0}^{negativebeta verticalaxis / negativealpha} e^{negativebeta^{2} verticalaxis^{2}} d horizontalaxis d verticalaxis+\int_{0}^{negativebeta} \int_{0}^{negativealpha horizontalaxis / negativebeta} e^{negativealpha^{2} horizontalaxis^{2}} d verticalaxis d horizontalaxis \\
& =\int_{0}^{negativealpha} \frac{negativebeta verticalaxis}{negativealpha} e^{negativebeta^{2} verticalaxis^{2}} d verticalaxis+\int_{0}^{negativebeta} \frac{negativealpha horizontalaxis}{negativebeta} e^{negativealpha^{2} horizontalaxis^{2}} d horizontalaxis \\
& =\int_{0}^{negativealpha^{2} negativebeta^{2}} \frac{1}{2 negativealpha negativebeta} e^{outputdummy} d outputdummy+\int_{0}^{negativealpha^{2} negativebeta^{2}} \frac{1}{2 negativealpha negativebeta} e^{initialdummy} d initialdummy \\
& =\frac{e^{negativealpha^{2} negativebeta^{2}}-1}{negativealpha negativebeta}
\end{aligned}
\]
>>>

garbled_string.question:
Evaluate
$\displaystyle{\int_0^{sxjqlfhe}\int_0^{ntrvpezo} e^{{\rm max}\{ntrvpezo^2 pkuwzoyd^2, sxjqlfhe^2 cgmhixra^2\}}\,d cgmhixra\,d pkuwzoyd}$
where $sxjqlfhe$ and $ntrvpezo$ are positive.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
* a,b > 0, * m,n > -1, * p,q > 0, * \kappa  > 1  

and set  
\sigma  := ln \kappa  > 0.  
Evaluate the double integral  

  I(a,b;m,n;p,q;\kappa ) := \int _0^a \int _0^b x^{m} y^{n} \kappa ^{\,\max\{\,b^{q}x^{p},\;a^{p}y^{q}\}} dy dx.

(When m = n = 0, p = q = 2 and \kappa  = e the original problem is recovered.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1 - Splitting the rectangle.  
The switching curve is obtained from  
b^{q}x^{p} = a^{p}y^{q} \Leftrightarrow  y = c x^{p/q}, where c := (b^{q}/a^{p})^{1/q}.  

Region R_1 (``below'' the curve): 0 \leq  y \leq  c x^{p/q}.  
Here max{\ldots }=b^{q}x^{p}.  

Region R_2 (``above'' the curve): c x^{p/q} \leq  y \leq  b.  
Here max{\ldots }=a^{p}y^{q}.  

I splits into I = I_1+I_2.

------------------------------------------------------------  
Step 2 - Region R_1.  
I_1 = \int _0^a \int _0^{c x^{p/q}} x^{m}y^{n} \kappa ^{\,b^{q}x^{p}} dy dx.

Inner integral  
 \int _0^{c x^{p/q}} y^{n} dy = c^{\,n+1}x^{(n+1)p/q}/(n+1).

Hence  
I_1 = c^{\,n+1}/[(n+1)] \int _0^a x^{m+(n+1)p/q} \kappa ^{\,b^{q}x^{p}} dx.

Put u := b^{q}x^{p} \Rightarrow  x = (b^{-q}u)^{1/p}, dx = u^{1/p-1}b^{-q/p}du/p.  
Let  

 \alpha  := (m+1)/p + (n+1)/q  (>0).  

Then  
x^{m+(n+1)p/q}dx = b^{-q\alpha }u^{\alpha -1}du/p.

Therefore  

I_1 = c^{\,n+1}b^{-q\alpha }/[(n+1)p] \int _0^{A} u^{\alpha -1} e^{\sigma u} du, A := a^{p}b^{q}.     (1)

------------------------------------------------------------  
Step 3 - Region R_2 (roles of x,y exchanged).  
Write the curve as x = d y^{q/p}, d := (a^{p}/b^{q})^{1/p}.  

I_2 = \int _0^b \int _0^{d y^{q/p}} x^{m}y^{n} \kappa ^{\,a^{p}y^{q}} dx dy.

Inner integral  
 \int _0^{d y^{q/p}} x^{m} dx = d^{\,m+1}y^{(m+1)q/p}/(m+1).

Proceed exactly as for I_1 using v := a^{p}y^{q}.  
One finds

I_2 = d^{\,m+1}a^{-p\alpha }/[(m+1)q] \int _0^{A} v^{\alpha -1} e^{\sigma v} dv.     (2)

------------------------------------------------------------  
Step 4 - Putting the constants together.  
Because  
c^{\,n+1} = (b^{q}/a^{p})^{(n+1)/q}, d^{\,m+1} = (a^{p}/b^{q})^{(m+1)/p},

a short calculation shows  

c^{\,n+1}b^{-q\alpha } = a^{-p(n+1)/q}b^{-q(m+1)/p},  
d^{\,m+1}a^{-p\alpha } = a^{-p(n+1)/q}b^{-q(m+1)/p}.  

Hence the same factor multiplies both integrals.  
Using the notation  

E_{\alpha }(z) := \int _0^{z} t^{\alpha -1}e^{t}dt (the ``generalised exponential integral''),

and observing that the integrals in (1) and (2) are identical (u \leftrightarrow  v), we get  

I = a^{-p(n+1)/q}b^{-q(m+1)/p} E_{\alpha }(\sigma A) \sigma ^{-\alpha } \cdot  [1/((n+1)p)+1/((m+1)q)].  

More compactly, with  

\alpha  = (m+1)/p + (n+1)/q, A = a^{p}b^{q}, \sigma  = ln \kappa ,

I(a,b;m,n;p,q;\kappa )  
  = [(m+1)q + (n+1)p]/[(m+1)(n+1)pq] \cdot  a^{-p(n+1)/q}b^{-q(m+1)/p}  
    \cdot  \sigma ^{-\alpha } E_{\alpha }(\sigma A).                                            (*)

------------------------------------------------------------  
Step 5 - Check with the original problem.  
Take m = n = 0, p = q = 2, \kappa  = e (\sigma  = 1).  
Then \alpha  = 1, the bracketed constant equals 1, and (*) gives  

I = a^{-1}b^{-1}(e^{a^{2}b^{2}}-1),  

which is exactly the original answer (with e-base).

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher-dimensional parameter space – Six independent positive parameters (a,b,m,n,p,q) plus the base κ make the integral far richer than the 2-parameter original.  
• Variable weights – The algebraic factors x^{m}y^{n} introduce Beta/Gamma–type behaviour and require controlling convergence at the axes.  
• Arbitrary powers – The exponents p,q force a non-linear switching curve y = c x^{p/q}, turning the simple straight-line partition of the rectangle into a curvilinear one.  
• Advanced special functions – The answer cannot be expressed with elementary functions; the generalised exponential integral (equivalently, incomplete gamma with a negative argument) is unavoidable.  
• Structural subtlety – A careful balance of exponents is needed to recognise that both sub-integrals lead to the same power α, enabling the final simplification.  
• Multiple interacting concepts – Change of variables, curvilinear region splitting, homogeneity considerations, and special-function identities must all be blended to reach the compact closed form.

These layers of complexity make the enhanced variant substantially harder than both the original problem and the current kernel variant, while still being fully solvable.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
* a,b > 0, * m,n > -1, * p,q > 0, * \kappa  > 1  

and set  
\sigma  := ln \kappa  > 0.

 \int _0^{d y^{q/p}} x^{m} dx = d^{\,m+1}y^{(m+1)q/p}/(m+1).

E_{\alpha }(z) := \int _0^{z} t^{\alpha -1}e^{t}dt (the ``generalised exponential integral''),
